era_context: The Classical Greek world in the aftermath of the Battle of Mantinea and the death of Epaminondas. No single Greek power dominates; Athens, Sparta, and Thebes compete for influence while remaining weakened by decades of warfare. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II faces succession challenges as the aging king approaches the end of his reign. The Roman Republic continues to consolidate power in central Italy. In China, the Warring States period continues.

events.title: Greek city-states compete for influence after Mantinea
events.description: In the year following the Battle of Mantinea, Greek city-states compete for influence in a fragmented political landscape. Athens, Sparta, and Thebes all seek to maintain or expand their power, but none is strong enough to dominate. This period is characterized by shifting alliances, diplomatic maneuvering, and ongoing military conflicts. The power vacuum created by Theban decline will eventually be filled by Macedon, but in 361 BCE, Macedon is not yet a major player in Greek politics.
events.certainty_note: The general political situation is well-attested, but specific events of 361 BCE are not always clearly distinguished from adjacent years.

events.title: Artaxerxes II Mnemon approaches end of reign
events.description: Artaxerxes II Mnemon, who has ruled the Persian Empire since 405 BCE, is now in his final years. The aging Great King faces succession challenges and internal instability. By 361 BCE, the question of succession is becoming increasingly pressing, as Artaxerxes II has multiple sons and the succession is not clearly established. The empire remains stable, but the transition of power will be contested.
events.certainty_note: The reign of Artaxerxes II is well-documented, but the exact timing of succession challenges is uncertain.

events.title: Philip II of Macedon begins consolidation of power
events.description: Philip II becomes king of Macedon around 359 BCE (exact date disputed) and begins a rapid consolidation of power. By 361 BCE, Philip is establishing himself as a major player in Macedonian politics, though he is not yet a significant force in broader Greek affairs. Philip's rise will eventually lead to Macedonian dominance of Greece and the creation of the Hellenistic world, but in 361 BCE, this future is not yet apparent.

sources.name: Plutarch, Life of Alexander (contains information about Philip)

events.certainty_note: The exact date of Philip II's accession to the throne is disputed, with sources ranging from 360 to 359 BCE. The events of 361 BCE in Macedon are not clearly attested.

events.title: Roman expansion in central Italy continues
events.description: The Roman Republic continues its gradual expansion and consolidation of power in central Italy. By 361 BCE, Rome has secured dominance over the Latin League and is beginning to extend influence into Campania. The internal conflict between patricians and plebeians continues, with the plebeian class gradually gaining political rights.
events.certainty_note: Roman history of this period is known primarily through Livy, whose sources are now lost. Specific events of 361 BCE are difficult to verify.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of 361 BCE as a distinct year in Greek history is minimal; it is primarily significant as the year after Mantinea. The rise of Philip II is sometimes portrayed as inevitable in later sources, but in 361 BCE, Philip's future dominance is not yet apparent. The exact date of Philip's accession to the throne is disputed among modern scholars.
historiographic_note: Sources for 361 BCE are primarily Greek and Persian accounts preserved in later compilations. The year 361 BCE is not particularly well-attested in any surviving source tradition; much of what is known about this year is inferred from broader patterns in the surrounding decades. The significance of this year lies primarily in what it leads to (the rise of Macedon) rather than in events that occurred during the year itself.

graph_edges.note: Greek political fragmentation continues until Macedon emerges as a dominant power
Artaxerxes II's approaching death leads to succession crisis
Philip II's consolidation of power leads to Macedonian dominance of Greece
